required.title: [Equipment] A basic fishing rod and reel (spinning setup)
[Book] Survivor Kid: A Practical Guide to Wilderness Survival
required.author: Denise Long

required.title: [Field Guide] A regional plant identification guide

suggested.title: [Book] The Kids' Guide to Fishing
suggested.author: Erin Hart or equivalent youth fishing guide

suggested.title: [Video] Your state fish and wildlife agency's youth fishing videos

supplementary.title: [Book] Look What I Found in the Woods
supplementary.author: Moira Butterfield

competence_markers.description: You can rig a basic fishing setup (hook, weight, bobber, bait) and cast to a target area
You have caught at least one fish, handled it yourself, and either released it properly or kept and processed it
You can read water at a basic level — point to where fish are likely to be and explain why
You have found and identified at least 5 animal signs in the field
You can identify dandelion, blackberry/raspberry, cattail, and one local edible plant with confidence
You can state and explain the foundational rule of foraging: if you are not absolutely certain, you do not eat it
You have kept a fishing log for at least 3 trips

narration_prompts.detail: What was the hardest part? What surprised you? How did it feel when you caught a fish (or how did it feel to wait a long time without catching one)?

narration_prompts.prompt: Where do fish live in the water?
narration_prompts.detail: Why do they choose certain spots? What did you notice about where you caught your fish?

narration_prompts.prompt: What animal signs did you find?
narration_prompts.detail: What species do you think made them? How do you know?

narration_prompts.prompt: Name the four edible wild plants you learned.
narration_prompts.detail: Describe each one — what does it look like? Where does it grow? What parts can you eat?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the most important rule of foraging?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is this rule so important? What could happen if you broke it?

narration_prompts.prompt: What does "patience" mean to you now
narration_prompts.detail: that you have been fishing? Has your understanding of patience changed?

cross_connections.description: Fishing is patience training. The student who sits for two hours without a bite and keeps fishing is building the same persistence that IC3.4 develops through controlled frustration.
Fire for cooking what you catch. The student who can catch a fish and cook it over a fire they built has closed the loop from raw material to meal.
Observing animal signs, reading water, and identifying plants are all applications of the observation and classification skills from 1.6.1.
Wildlife habitat connects to regional geography. The student who understands their local ecosystem understands why certain fish, animals, and plants are found where they are.

Handling a living creature — fish or plant — with respect is a character formation experience. Food costs something. The student who has caught and kept a fish understands this at a level that grocery shopping cannot reach.

content.why_this_matters:
Every piece of food you have ever eaten came from something that was alive. The chicken nugget was a chicken. The apple grew on a tree. The bread started as wheat growing in a field. Most people never think about this because food comes from a store, wrapped in plastic, looking nothing like the living thing it came from.

You are going to be different. You are going to catch a fish from a river or a lake — a real, living fish — and you are going to understand that this is where food comes from. Not from a store. Not from a factory. From the natural world, through patience, skill, and respect for the animals and plants that sustain human life.

You are also going to learn about wild plants you can eat — plants that grow in fields and forests and even in your backyard, free for anyone who knows what to look for. And you are going to learn the most important rule in foraging, the one rule that keeps you safe: **if you are not absolutely certain, you do not eat it.**

This packet is about patience. Fishing teaches patience better than almost anything else in the world. You will sit, and wait, and watch the water, and sometimes the fish will not come. That is part of it. The patience you learn here will serve you in every other part of your life.
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Where Food Really Comes From

Before you pick up a fishing rod, think about this: for most of human history — hundreds of thousands of years — every person on earth got their food by hunting animals, catching fish, and gathering wild plants. There were no stores. There were no farms. If you wanted to eat, you went out and found food, or you did not eat.

That changed when people learned to farm — about 10,000 years ago. Farming was one of the most important changes in human history. But for a very long time before that, and in many places even after farming began, people relied on wild food.

Today, almost nobody needs to hunt or fish or forage to survive. But the people who can do these things understand something that other people do not: food is not free. It comes from living things. Getting it takes skill, patience, and respect for the animals and plants that provide it.

When you catch a fish, you are connecting to the oldest human skill. Every one of your ancestors — every single one, going back to the beginning — caught food from the wild. You are continuing something that has never stopped.

### Part 2: Fishing — Your First Wild Food Skill

Fishing is the best starting point for wild food because it is accessible (most places have fishable water), it is legal for children in most states without a license (check your local regulations), and it teaches patience in a way nothing else does.

**Your fishing setup:**

- **Rod and reel:** A spinning rod, 5-6 feet long. The reel sits on the bottom of the rod. You hold the rod with the reel hanging below your hand.
- **Line:** The reel comes pre-loaded with monofilament line. For now, this is fine.
- **Hook:** A single hook, size 6-10 (smaller number = bigger hook; size 8 is a good starting point for panfish like bluegill, perch, and crappie).
- **Weight:** A split-shot sinker, pinched onto the line about 8-12 inches above the hook. This carries your bait down to the fish.
- **Bobber:** A small round or pencil bobber, clipped to the line 2-3 feet above the hook. The bobber floats on the surface and tells you when a fish is biting — when it dips or moves, something is pulling on your hook.
- **Bait:** Worms. Live worms are the most universally effective bait for beginning fishing. Thread the worm onto the hook, covering the hook point but leaving some worm dangling to attract fish.

**How to cast:**

1. Hold the rod with your dominant hand. The reel handle should point to the side.
2. With a spinning reel: open the bail (the metal arm that wraps line around the spool) with your other hand. Hold the line against the rod with your index finger.
3. Point the rod tip at your target area.
4. Bring the rod back behind your shoulder and swing it forward smoothly — not hard, smooth.
5. Release the line from your finger when the rod tip is pointing at your target. The weight and bait fly out over the water.
6. Close the bail by turning the handle.

**This takes practice.** Your first casts will go short, go sideways, or tangle. That is normal. Practice on a lawn before going to the water if you want — tie a small weight on the end and cast at a target.

### Part 3: Reading the Water

Fish do not live everywhere in a lake or river. They live in specific places, for specific reasons. Learning to read water — to look at a body of water and figure out where the fish are — is one of the most important fishing skills.

**In a pond or lake:**
- Fish gather near structure — submerged logs, rocks, weed beds, docks, fallen trees. Structure provides hiding places from predators and ambush points for prey.
- Shaded areas are often better than open sun, especially in warm weather.
- Drop-offs (where shallow water suddenly becomes deep) concentrate fish.

**In a river or stream:**
- Fish hold behind rocks and logs — the current is slower there, so the fish uses less energy while food drifts past.
- Bends in the river create slower water on the inside of the bend and faster water on the outside. Fish often hold on the inside.
- Undercut banks (where the water has eroded under the bank) are excellent fish habitat.

**The key idea:** Fish are not random. They are where conditions are best for them — food, shelter, and comfortable water temperature. Learn to think like a fish, and you will find fish.

### Part 4: Catching, Handling, and Keeping or Releasing

**When the bobber goes down:**
- Wait a moment — let the fish take the bait. Do not jerk immediately.
- Then set the hook — lift the rod tip firmly with a quick upward motion. Not a massive yank. A firm lift.
- Reel in steadily. Keep the rod tip up. If the fish pulls hard, let it take some line rather than forcing it — you will break the line if you fight a strong fish with a tight drag.

**Handling the fish:**
- Wet your hands before touching a fish. Dry hands remove the protective slime coating that keeps fish healthy.
- Hold the fish firmly but gently. Many panfish have small spines in their dorsal fin — approach from the front, sliding your hand from head to tail to lay the spines flat.
- Remove the hook carefully. If the hook is deep, use needle-nose pliers or a hook remover.

**Keeping or releasing:**
- If you are keeping the fish to eat (and it meets the legal size and bag limits for your area), dispatch it quickly and humanely — a sharp blow to the top of the head with a rock or the back of a knife. A quick death is the respectful way.
- If you are releasing the fish, handle it as little as possible, remove the hook gently, and release it back into the water facing into the current so water flows over its gills.

### Part 5: Animal Signs — Reading the Landscape

Even before you learn to hunt, you can learn to see what animals are doing around you. Most animals are invisible most of the time — they are hiding, sleeping, or moving when you are not around. But they leave signs everywhere.

**Tracks:**
- Learn to identify the tracks of 5 common animals in your area. Start with: deer, raccoon, rabbit, squirrel, and one bird (turkey or heron are easy to identify).
- Look for tracks in mud, soft dirt, sand, or snow.
- Notice the direction of travel — which way was the animal going?

**Scat (animal droppings):**
- Deer scat looks like small dark pellets in a cluster.
- Rabbit scat is small round balls, lighter in color.
- Raccoon scat is often found on logs or rocks (they use regular "latrine" spots).
- Scat tells you an animal was here recently and what it was eating.

**Other signs:**
- Browse lines — where deer have eaten all the leaves up to a certain height on shrubs
- Rubbed trees — where bucks have rubbed their antlers, stripping bark
- Trails — paths through grass or underbrush worn by repeated animal travel
- Digging — where animals have dug for roots, grubs, or burrowing

**Go for a walk with your mentor and look for these signs.** You will be amazed at how much animal activity is happening around you once you know what to look for.

### Part 6: Wild Plants You Can Eat — The Foundational Rule

**THE RULE:** If you are not absolutely certain what a plant is, you do not eat it. Period. No exceptions.

Some plants that look edible are poisonous. Some are deadly. One wrong identification can make you very sick or kill you. This is not a game or a guessing activity. You must be 100% certain before you eat any wild plant. When in doubt, do not eat it.

With that rule firmly in place, here are four plants that are so distinctive and so widely distributed that you can learn to identify them with confidence:

**Dandelion:**
- Yellow flower on a hollow stem, turning into a white puffball of seeds. Leaves are jagged and form a rosette at ground level.
- Every part is edible: leaves (raw in salad or cooked like spinach), flowers (raw or fried), roots (roasted and ground for a coffee substitute).
- Found in lawns, fields, and roadsides across North America.
- No dangerous look-alikes at the mature stage.

**Blackberries and Raspberries:**
- Thorny canes with compound leaves (3-5 leaflets). Berries are compound (made of many small segments called drupelets).
- Ripe berries are edible — blackberries are dark purple-black, raspberries are red (or black for black raspberries). Both are unmistakable when ripe.
- Found along forest edges, fence rows, and disturbed areas.

**Cattail:**
- Tall, grass-like plant with a distinctive brown, cigar-shaped seed head. Grows in marshes, pond edges, and ditches.
- Called the "supermarket of the swamp" because so many parts are edible: young shoots (peeled, eaten raw like cucumber), pollen (collected and used as flour), roots (starchy, peeled and cooked).
- No dangerous look-alikes when the seed head is present. *Caution:* before the seed head appears, cattail leaves resemble iris leaves — iris is toxic. Wait for the seed head to confirm identification.

**One additional local species — chosen with your mentor:**
- Your mentor will help you identify one more edible plant in your specific area. Possibilities include: wood sorrel (small clover-like plant with a sour, lemony taste), plantain (the weed, not the banana — broad, ribbed leaves found in lawns), or another species appropriate to your region.

**Hands-on practice:** You will go into the field with your mentor and identify each of these plants in their natural habitat. You will harvest at least one and eat it. This is not a textbook exercise. You will hold the plant, examine it, confirm its identity, and eat it — making the connection between wild landscape and food.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### Fishing Setup and Casting

**Developing well:**
- Can thread line through rod guides, tie a hook (with an acceptable knot — clinch knot or similar), add weight and bobber, and bait the hook with minimal assistance
- Casts to within a reasonable distance of the intended target area (does not need to be precise — within 10 feet of the target is fine)
- Retrieves line smoothly without major tangles
- Can adjust bobber depth when asked or when fishing is not producing results

**Developing slowly:**
- Cannot assemble the rig without step-by-step guidance each time
- Casts go wildly off target or result in frequent tangles
- Does not attempt to adjust approach (same cast to the same spot despite no results)

### Fish Handling

**Developing well:**
- Wets hands before handling the fish
- Holds the fish firmly without squeezing
- Assists with or completes hook removal (pliers use may require mentor help for small children)
- Makes the keep-or-release decision and executes release properly (or assists with dispatch and keeping)
- Does not show excessive squeamishness — some initial discomfort is normal, but by the third or fourth fish, handling should be matter-of-fact

**Developing slowly:**
- Refuses to touch the fish
- Drops the fish repeatedly
- Cannot overcome squeamishness after multiple exposures

**Note:** Some children are initially reluctant to touch fish. This is normal. Do not force it on the first trip — let them watch you, then encourage them to try on the second or third trip. Persistent refusal after 4-5 exposures may indicate a deeper aversion that needs gentle, patient work.

### Water Reading

**Developing well:**
- Can point to structure (logs, rocks, weed beds, docks) and explain why fish might be there
- Notices differences between areas of water (shaded vs. sunny, deep vs. shallow, still vs. moving)
- Makes casting decisions based on water reading ("I'm going to cast near that log because fish like to hide there")

**Developing slowly:**
- Casts randomly with no thought to where fish might be
- Cannot identify structure in the water even when pointed out
- Does not connect fish location to habitat features

### Animal Signs

**Developing well:**
- Finds at least 5 signs on a walk without being shown where to look
- Can identify the species responsible for at least 3 signs
- Shows genuine curiosity — looks for more, asks questions about what they find
- Notices signs independently in other settings ("Dad, look — deer tracks!")

**Developing slowly:**
- Only finds signs when you point them out
- Cannot differentiate between types of tracks or scat
- Shows no curiosity beyond the exercise

### Wild Plant Identification

**Developing well:**
- Can identify all four target species in the field by multiple characteristics (not just "it looks like the picture")
- States the foundational rule without prompting when encountering an unknown plant
- Harvests and eats at least one wild plant with appropriate confidence (not reckless, not terrified)
- Can describe what part of each plant is edible

**Developing slowly:**
- Identifies plants only from memory of a single characteristic (color, for example) without checking other features
- Does not apply the foundational rule — willing to eat plants they are not certain about
- Refuses to eat any wild plant even after confirmed identification (gentle encouragement is appropriate; forcing is not)
